comments.title: Quick Question for JMG, et al
comments.content:
JMG et al,

I'd like to get your opinion on one of those episodes in life that likely isn't all that rare. Mainly any thoughts on
the "esoteric" implications of this issue, i.e., so-called "karmic" implications if you think this might have
generated any? Did the big "scorekeeper" in the sky make a note of this? Somehow I don't think so?

A while back I loaned a "friend" some money to help him out of one of those tight times life throws at us.
Several hundred dollars in fact. There were no formalities, documentation of any kind, sadly no way to prove anything,
just pay me back ASAP when you can. All went well for a while and I got a couple of payments, but not everything, then
I heard nothing for some time, and no further payback. I was able to contact his employer who informed me that he had
moved out of town but wouldn't supply any details.

So, I was stuck with everything that implies. At first I was angry, but as time went on just plain sad.
No..., I have not considered any form of active retribution, but I'd like to get some independent thoughts on how this
might shake out on other planes, might this "carry over" at some point?

Thanks for any thoughts on the implications of something like this!

replies.title: Re: Quick Question for JMG, et al
replies.content:
Okay, let's start with some basics. Karma is not a matter of some "big scorekeeper in the sky." Karma is simply the process by which every thought, word, and action produces consequences. You loaned some money to an untrustworthy person; that had consequences. You're now feeling various emotions as a result; those have consequences, and so on.

The person who ripped you off also generated consequences by that action, and those consequences will combine with other thoughts, words, and actions to shape his future life and lives. Nothing is ever forgotten; neither you nor anyone else ever gets away with anything; what you do shapes who you are, and who you are shapes what you become and what you experience.

So the question I'd encourage you to reflect on is this: what will you do in response to this experience? How will it affect you, and what will you do about that?

replies.title: Re: Quick Question for JMG, et al
replies.content:
I've been thinking about karma and the law of consequences in relation to the current catastrophe going on in the M.E.
You have mentioned previously that if you can deal with so called 'bad' karma with as much grace and dignity you can muster, this can be of great benefit.
You also mentioned that to help someone else out in dealing with their 'bad' karma is also beneficial.

It makes me think that if groups of people or even nations were able to deal with the trauma/bad karma of their ancestors with more good grace, and also not inflict more 'bad' karmic inducing consequences on themselves via the treatment of others in a somewhat similar fashion, (a bitter irony, methinks), then, it could maybe be possible to break the cycle of tragedy?

I know you've mentioned before that forgiveness is not the be all and end all, but can it be used in a way to break or at least lesson the severity of the law of consequences?

Do you get what I'm trying to say?

One other thing,I keep thinking about that post you wrote many years ago in response to a reader, and that you have linked to a few times since.
The one about the West's most taboo subject ie, "thou shall never criticize" and what happened the last time the benefactor money ran out.

I've noticed that a lot of mainly right leaning commentators who constantly talk about free speech and being able to speak without being 'cancelled' have said nary a word on the subject.
I actually think that in the months or perhaps years to come some of these people will go through a personal crisis.
They know they should be saying something, but for various reasons, they are too afraid to.
Including being 'cancelled' by their 'side'.
I may be wrong, but it just seems like the silence is deafening - to me anyway.

Note: When I say, 'the subject" , I mean that of the first paragraph (M.E. catastrophe), not your blog post.

replies.title: Re: Quick Question for JMG, et al
replies.content:
1) Yes, I understand what you're saying, and you're quite correct. What keeps the doom loop going in the Middle East, and will keep it going until the last Israeli and the last Palestinian die with their hands around each other's throats, is a familiar but hopelessly wrongheaded idea: "The wrongs that I suffer justify the wrongs that I commit." Forgiveness is one way to break out of that loop; there are plenty of others, but they all require a willingness to set, and maintain, a higher ethical standard than the other guy.

2) No question, the silence is deafening. It's left to fringe thinkers like me to point out where all this is headed.

replies.title: Re: Quick Question for JMG, et al
replies.content: I'm not the original poster. Would you have suggestions to a male for a cure for severe /pol/ poisoning on the nature of women? It took me a bit of effort to write women without an asterisk, like it was a bad word.

replies.title: Re: Quick Question for JMG, et al
replies.content:
Sure. Start by spending much less time on /pol/ and its equivalents, as spending time there will feed the imbalance. Next, read books by dead people -- preferably people who have been dead for a long time -- to help jolt you out of the current cultural mindset. After that...well, that's where it gets sticky.

Remember that "divide and conquer" is the basic strategy of every tyrant. One way that's been done most disastrously in our current situation is that men and women have been systematically set against each other. Women and men have both been taught to act in ways that alienate the other gender, and women and men have also both been taught to misinterpret the actions of the other gender in ways that reinforce the alienation. Rinse and repeat, and you've got an entire society where men and women are at each other's throats most of the time, while corporate advertising and mass media hammer on images of heterosexual love and sex. If you want to keep people in a state of seething anger, confusion, frustration, and misery, so they can't think clearly and are easy to manipulate, it's a great scheme.

Getting out of that isn't a fast process. It can be done, but it requires you to grapple with the fact that all your life you've been fed a pack of lies about women, sex, and relationships, and that women have been fed a different (but equally toxic) pack of lies about the same thing. One way you can get a sense of the programming they're getting is to go to the library and pick up a couple of recent romance novels. (Tell the librarian your girlfriend is sick and asked you to get them.) Pay close attention to the bizarre caricature of masculinity who stars as the male lead -- then compare it to the bizarre caricatures of femininity who feature in pornography. (Romance fiction is emotional porn.) Notice how both caricatures set up expectations that no human being can meet -- and think about how those expectations guarantee failure in most relationships.

There's more, but we can talk about it later. Give those steps a try and see where they get you.

replies.title: help-a-friend loans
replies.content:
I've been on both sides of help-a-friend loans.

I think it's vital that you don't loan more than you could afford to lose. Maybe the borrower is sincere, but tough times could continue. Maybe other problems from the borrower would mean no payback.

I also think it's vital to write it up. Who is lender, who is borrower, amount, date, expected payment plan or "as soon as." Both people sign. This is to make sure you agree at the start.

Use it to sue? Probably useless. Even if you knew where he was, a broke person wouldn't have money for a judgment. If you get money from a trip to court, legal fees and hassles and broken friendship may make any recovered money worthless and bitter to you.

You weren't willing to keep score on earth, when it would have only taken a few minutes to do so. Why should a scorekeeper in the sky make a note, when you literally didn't care enough to make a note on earth?

Hope your friend's job wasn't Maintenance Engineer for WJLX-AM in Jasper, Alabama. If it was, he ain't never coming back to town.

replies.title: Re: help-a-friend loans
replies.content:
ditto, most of this.

Personal policy on lending: It's a gift. Keep it. Get your life in order. If, at some point in the future, you're in a position to gift it back, I will gladly accept it and say thank you, but... I'm not expecting it, and I won't hate you if it doesn't happen. Don't be weird about it. I love you.

And like the respondent above: if you can't afford to lose it, don't lend it.

replies.title: Re: help-a-friend loans
replies.content:
Absolutely this. Don't ever expect to get it back. You don't have to say that to them when you give it, but you know in your heart it's a gift to them and that's the end of it. And if they do pay it back, why, that's a gift to both of you because you got to get your money back, and they feel good about paying it back!

I've been on both sides of this equation and I'm sure my ex-girlfriend had long forgotten about it when I finally got on my feet and sent her a check for over $3,000. (I did have a paper record of what was owed, BTW.)

For the situation initially described--maybe because I grew up with different currencies (USD, the furball of european currencies before the Euro, the Rupee, the UAE doofers [that's what we called it anyway]etc) that what "money" actually represents, in real terms, becomes very unstable)--I default to a principle my depression era parents had: Geld kommt wieder.

replies.title: Re: TCM and kundalini; tarot
replies.content:
I'm familiar enough with TCM to say that's an excellent article. I haven't had a kundalini awakening.

The experience of the author's partner sounds terrifying.

This passage from the article is a good description of PTSD: "What is left, and what we care about, is survival and not being killed because we are really left with the limbic system of survival when our executive functioning goes. We have the fear responses of anxiety, hypervigilance, paranoia, and a feeling that something is negative and wrong."

Exercise and diet programs often recommend you check with your doctor before you begin.
Maybe it would be smart for people who want to pursue a kundalini awakening to find a TCM practitioner, who is familiar with kundalini. (Should be easy to ask in a quick call.) Get the yin/yang balance and any weak energy systems looked at before they start.

As for your tarot reading, here are some questions you could consider, if you aren't familiar with how to set up your own discursive meditation.

Six of Pentacles shows a person giving to the needy.
If it's 6 of pentacles for "me," where are you in the picture? Are you the donor? Are you among the needy receiving help? Are you a bystander?
The donor carries scales. Are the scales used in his donations, or are they unrelated? The scales are shown in balance. If he's using them for his charity, how does that work?
The background has a castle or city. Is it the donor's home? There are trees, but at a constructed horizon. Are they in a park? Is the line a road at the edge of town? How does the plant life relate to the charity scene?

Continue with the other cards and then consider their links. What is the relationship between the charitable giver and the Magician with his spiritual implements or his bag of tricks? Why did you get two cards with a man taking action and in between them, a woman?

If it's The World for "situation," what does the picture of ultimate success of "winning the world" say about the situation? That would look more like a philanthropist than a beggar, wouldn't it?

If it's Magician for "outcome," who is practicing magic? Is this the result of worldly success? Is the magician hoping to achieve a situation of worldly success?

Hope these help spark some of your own meditations that relate to your own message received.

Mocha Amphibious Moose

replies.title: Re: q
replies.content:
I'm going to comment on the "how to heal kundalini syndrome" post and linked article. I don't know if what I experienced is exactly the same thing, and I have no idea if I had "too much yin". My experiences when I was doing a lot of "mindful" concentration practices, preparing for and participating in various extended retreats. Although the first time I think I experienced this was when I had just started reading some of JMG's material on middle pillar, pentagrams, etc. and -- with an abundance of ignorance about what I was actually doing, and how to do things "correctly" -- I tried to ... well, I tried to force myself into the astral plane after a few minutes of settling /concentrating the mind with breathing exercises.

Okay. So here is what happened to me, as best I can recall it. The first time I remember opening my eyes and seeing all sorts of lightening flashing above my head. Ego maniac that I am/was I actully figured I was getting somewhere and pushed harder upward, encouraging the lightening.

What happened next was that it felt like something demonic dropped out of the sky and into my body For a fraction of a second I felt like I wasn't in control of my body but felt as if I was possessed. I felt, very suddenly, suicidal. Just for a second or two. Then I felt the presence lift and I felt I was me, again, and back in the world. I was so spooked by the experience that I actually called a friend and asked if I could come over and spend the night on her sofa. I tried to explain what I had experienced and she gave me some sage to smudge the house when I came back home the next day. That helped.

So I didn't try THAT again. But I WAS preparing for a two month retreat and I was supposed to be practicing Buddhist meditation. What I noticed was that the more I meditated, the more depressed I became. And the depression came on in the aftermath of the sessions and persisted for weeks.

Only when I discontinued regular mindfulness meditation did it, gradually (over a period of weeks) start to gradually lift. When I later went back to practicing regular meditation, the depression gradually returned.

I'm not really sure what to make of all that. I had done a lot of DEEP trance work for decades before that and never had any problems. Indeed, I felt the deep trance work, especially the parts work, was relaxing and helped me function better and feel better.

Somehow what I was doing was NOT healthy. I tried mindfulness meditation, and other deep Buddhist (Zen and Tibetan) practices, on and off after that. (yeah, I can be stubborn) Every time, when I practiced too much the depression started creeping back in.

What helped? Well, as I said, the first time the smudging helped. After that, I found that I just had to STOP meditating and, over weeks, the depression which crept in would gradually lift. Spending time in nature, and taking walks by the sea seemed to speed up my recovery back to happiness.

I used to think that trance states were more or less all variations on a theme. They definitely ARE related; there are a lot of similarities between the doorway into deep jhanic states and going deep into trance to do something like automatic writing. But these states are ALSO quite different. And mindfulness meditation doesn't always produce happiness and bliss, the way it is explained in the books. And, yes, I think some people are okay with one type of trance and not with the other.

It really bothers me, because even now there is a part of me that wants to go deep and continue exploring the deep realms of meditation. But I've resisted that urge for the last few years. I am more inclined to get back to my self hypnosis / trance practices which don't involve force of will nor such deep concentration.

John? Any comments on what I am describing? I didn't used to believe in demons. But now I kinda sorta do!

P.S. I am not trying to discourage anyone from Buddhist meditation. Nor any other use of altered state. But I think one needs to back off when things are not making you feel better or, otherwise, getting a bit too weird and feeling unsafe. I DO think Kundalini Yoga has a much higher likelihood of producing this sort of ab-reaction.

In insight meditation they talk about "Dark Night of the Soul" as the ego starts to realize that "it" isn't a thing. It is commonly encountered, I've been told, during normal insight practice. With some people, they get through it quickly. With some people it can last some years but, supposedly, will gradually lift with persistent practice. So I have been told.

Maybe if I did things "right" I wouldn't have had these experiences and I would be happily meditating still. But I am, at heart, a hacker. And I am drawn to the idea that if someone can do something with their head, I should be able to do it too. And maybe I really could work my way through to "enlightenment" via that path. But, really, the aftermath of the deeper experiences suggest otherwise. And my various gurus, who seemingly mastered all this, didn't have much to suggest when I told them what happened to me.

Hmmm. Maybe I should find a Chinese doctor to boost up my yang, and I can try again?

replies.title: Re: q
replies.content:
Mindfulness meditation is a watered-down version of one form of Buddhist meditation. As practiced in the West, it's pulled out of its context as one part of a whole system of spiritual practice, and thus is very vulnerable to imbalance. Experiences like yours are far from rare, and rather too often have ended in suicide or severe mental illness. Thus I don't encourage you to go back to that practice.

There are other forms of meditation that don't have the same problem. The method taught in my books, discursive meditation, seems to be entirely safe. It won't get you into deep trances, but it's a gentle, effective, healthy practice that leads to increased wisdom, thoughtfulness, and clarity; forty years of daily practice in it have done me a lot of good.

Oh, and demons certainly exist. A mind that's empty of thought can be easy pickings for them, which is one of the reasons why most Western occult teachers have their students do a protective working before each session of meditation.

comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer, a geomantic question, if you will.

My partner and I are in the process of moving our family from Israel to Europe (probably Lisbon) in the coming months, and as part of this process we put the apartment that we own here up for sale. This made my father in law really worried, saying that we should balance the desire to quickly save our property from the burning house with caution of putting all our capital in money form ahead of a complicated, unpredictable migration. He strongly recommended that we first settle in a new place, then sell.

So we asked the oracle if this is a good time for us to sell the apartment. I have two questions regarding the reply we got.

1. Our first (right) witness was Puella, our second (left) witness was Cauda Draconis, and the judge was Acquisitio. The medieval geomancy guide we consulted (attributed to Ibn Ezra) regards this trio as unfavourable for financial questions. Would you agree in this case? And in what way? Acquisitio as a judge would sound promising to me.

2. We also had Cauda Draconis placed in three of the houses, including the first and second ones. This we were unsure how to interpret, because on one hand it is regarded as extremely malefic (Stephen Skinner goes as far as writing “if this symbol appears in the first house, the divination should be abandoned and the forms destroyed”), but on the other hand it may be beneficial for ending things, which is after all what we are trying to do. What do you think?

After also consulting the tarot (with which I feel much more at home) we decided to postpone selling the apartment. But for educational purposes I’m interested in your take on this reading.

Thanks,
Omer

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Cauda Drac in the first house indicates that by the time you did the divination, you'd already made up your mind what to do. Other than that, well, I don't use Ibn Ezra's methods so can't really comment on them; I'd have taken that set of figures as a mildly positive answer, and interpreted the whole reading as saying, "Yes, this would be a good time, but you've already decided not to."

If we had your interpretation in front of us at the time we would have taken it to mean that we already know the answer and should just go ahead with the sale. But then the CD scared the sh*t out of me, along with Ibn Ezra's unfavorable interpretation...

I guess magically this would be a good job for a course of study that works with a complete set of forces evenly like the four elements or the planets so you can gradually grow all your other abilities up to par, and its a good reason to avoid focusing on a single force you happen to like. comments.title: Thoughts on Religious Authenticity and Question on Zerohedge Article on Theosophy and Satanism
comments.content:
Good Evening,

I hope everyone is having a good week, and that the warming spring weather is welcome!

To Share: A comment on last week's Magic Monday led me to some thoughts on how to approach polytheistic worship in the face of the challenges given by the modern world: https://jpowellrussell.com/#further_thoughts_on_religious_authenticity

To Ask: I recently read this Zerohedge Article which asserts a clear throughline from Theosophy to modern Luciferianism, and attributes the latter to WEF globalist types. I'd normally dismiss this as ignorant scaremongering about Occultism, but the article includes some quotes of Blavatsky defending "Lucifer" as a different figure than the devil, and ultimately a sympathetic one. So, I suppose my question is a. how much link would you say there is between 19th century Theosophy and 20th-21st century Satanism, and b. whatever link there is or isn't, do you think 19th century Theosophist thinking on "Lucifer" fell into glorifying what was overall a negative spiritual force?

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Religious Authenticity and Question on Zerohedge Article on Theosophy and Satanism
replies.content: One of Steiner's most interesting habits was the way that he took ideas from early Theosophy and redefined them in more useful ways. For Steiner, Lucifer is one of the two powers of evil in the cosmos (the other one is Ahriman); both are always present in human consciousness, and both have to be brought into balance under the Christ impulse, mediated in this age of the world by the archangel Michael.

replies.content: Yes, I read the same clueless article. Blavatsky liked to get the goat of Christians by saying provocative things about Lucifer; the Theosophical Society very sensibly dropped that habit like a hot rock the moment she died, and in fact played an important role in helping to spread independent Christian churches. There's essentially no link between Theosophy and the current crop of pseudo-Satanist clowns; the latter can trace their lineage straight back to Howard Stanton Levey aka Anton Szandor LaVey, one of the great put-on artists of the 20th century, who found that playacting devil worship got Jayne Mansfield to take off her clothes in his living room. As for the early Theosophical use of the name "Lucifer," it was a dumb move, no question, and yes, it ended up encouraging some people to glorify the "rebel without a clue" mentality we see so much of today.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Religious Authenticity and Question on Zerohedge Article on Theosophy and Satanism
replies.content:
Oh, granted, many of the same people who are attracted to Levey and the Satanism he set in motion tend to glorify Crowley too. Levey and Crowley both did their level best to embody the "bad boy" image, and both of them launched traditions in which bare female flesh plays an important role in ritual and sex is at least an implied benefit of joining -- thus they draw on the same catchment basin of potential customers.

Don't make the mistake of equating Crowley with the Golden Dawn, though. Yes, he was a member and got some of his magical training there, but they threw him out -- and for good reason. The magical (or "magickal") system that he created once he was on his own was a dog's breakfast drawn from just about every magical, mystical, and avant-garde tradition of his time, so long as it rejected Christianity and embraced sexual excess. The Golden Dawn, by contrast, is full of Christian symbolism and had a far more restrained notion of proper behavior for adepts!

comments.title: Calcination, cont'd
comments.content:
Some months ago you and open_source gave me advice about calcination. I’d like to report back, with my thanks, and ask one further question. Efforts with a small barbecue bell were unsuccessful, probably because I never learned how to get charcoal burning properly; but I got decent results by placing the crucibles in a fireplace during long winter fires on the hearth, where the glowing embers would bury the crucible. It required many hours, though.

Here is a picture of the salts I obtained from the burnt residue of Rosemary.

https://tinyurl.com/2adctdb9

Although the quantity of the end product is small, I have found that it is more than will dissolve into the tincture. This intrigues me, since some authors suggest that reuniting the salts with the sulphur and mercury restores a natural whole in the right proportions.

I’m still finding that some herbs calcine more readily than others. Here is a picture of the ashes of Burdock Root (on the left) and Rosemary (on the right) after being treated together in the same 8 or 9 fires.

https://tinyurl.com/yfv86ae3

My question: a few weeks ago, the commenter not_gandalf said one should be cautious about ingesting the salts obtained when making spagyric tinctures, because they are vegetable alkaloids and may be toxic. That got my attention! I had not heard this before. How could I learn — other than by experimenting — which herbs pose a risk in this way?

replies.title: Re: Calcination, cont'd
replies.content: That's why you add only as much ashes to the sulphur and mercury as the latter can absorb, and then take the resulting product in very small doses -- an amount the size of a pinhead is quite adequate. That's also why you only make alchemical magisteries from herbs that are safe to ingest in their normal form. If you follow those rules the very small amounts of alkaloids remaining in the calcinated ash will be enough to stimulate your immune system and your body generally, but not enough to hurt you in any way.

replies.title: Re: Calcination, cont'd
replies.content:
Hey! Those rosemary salts look pretty nice!
The way I used these things was as follows: I took the salt I obtained (using leeching with distilled water as the final process) and added it to the original tincture, and allowed the whole thing to digest again. Then it filtered and took the resulting physical material (the salts which had absorbed further impurities from the tincture) and calcined and leeched again until they were white. I did this several times and finally just said "enough" and added the salts back to the tincture (it was melissa oficianalis, BTW). My friend, who was running the Philosophers of Nature at the time, said that the salts would act as a "magnet" for the impurities on the tincture, progressively getting stronger, and ultimately, the tincture would become clear. I just took it to the point where I felt I had a potent Jupiter charged plant tincture and called it a day.
As to poisonous alkaloids, I believe there are lists of known dangerous ones. I think nicotine, salts from tobacco are among the bad actors.

comments.title: past lives

Once more, I offer condolences to what you're going through. I also forgot to mention that it was rather interesting to have a fuller profile of the late great Sara, who until recently was a vague yet positive presence here.

You also took a shot at the New Age movement, which always struck me as cobbled-together gypsy wagon of truths, half-truths, and falsehoods. As I've mentioned before, my oldest past-life memory, and the most vivid, dates back to centuries ago, I believe the medieval period. It feels distant in time somehow, I know it's well before 1800, but not from antiquity; that and other clues leads me to believe it's around the Mongol conquests, although I'm not certain -- it could've been the Jin-Song conflict, or another flare-up in medieval East Asia; there are a few different candidates for the major war that happened when I/she was older.

I'm not sure how long I've been in the human world, but I believe it's been at least 800 years, and possibly longer. How does this compare overall? Am I messing up, or doing well? I believe some of my more recent lives ended in violent death, I remember drowning when the Titanic sunk, and I may have died in WWII, although I don't remember that and I'm just going by a hunch. The medieval life I remember best was happier and healthier overall, whereas in this life I've dealt with depression, and I'm now on meds for malignant hypertension, a condition which nearly killed me. I'm wondering how all this factors in to my karmic credit/debt, and how much is the workings of chance. comments.title: Dreams
comments.content:
Morning JMG!

I had a realization about dreams the other day. The part of my brain that does dreams has no connection to the part of my brain that stores memories.

I remember many of my dreams and I'm used to the fact that the images in them get a bit distorted from reality. But I got stuck recently in one of those waking loops where I think I'm awake but I'm still dreaming and saw something that I began noticing in all my dreams.

I would wake up and think thank goodness I'm back in my bed and I'm awake. After looking around for a second I realized my bedroom did not have anything familiar in it. I would then immediately wake up and think thank goodness I'm back in my bed and I'm awake only to once again see the unfamiliarity.

There was the moment where I said this is my bedroom and believed it. But I see now I was projecting on to the image in front of me.

After seeing this I have noticed in every subsequent dream and the large catalog of dreams I remember. They don't have a single piece of familiarity to them. The locations are all wrong but I project the places I know onto them. The dramatis personae are all wrong but I once again project onto them who I think they need to be and make it a point of not looking too closely at them so the assumption can remain intact.

So two possibilities. As I said the dream brain and memory brain don't communicate. Or dreams are entirely external and I paint them with local color so they can take on importance to me like a radio station playing random noises that I hear and say that's one of my favorites and sing along to something that is clearly not there.

Maybe there isn't a question here but it seems unsettling to think that every night I tune into a stream of imagery of unknown source and pretend that it's home and wife and parent etc.

I have seen scenes of intense grandeur in my dreams and have every time said "this equals this" instead of dealing with the Lovecraftian possibility that I have seen something completely unfamiliar and possibly far stranger.

replies.title: Re: Dreams
replies.content:
I'd suggest that maybe this isn't so much a matter of either/or, but both, sometimes at the same time. I'd say some of your dreams of people you know could be the astral version of them, and you really are connecting to them. Sometimes it might be symbolic however.

One of the most striking series of recurrent dreams I had was where I was going down into these sewer systems deep within the woods I played in childhood. In one of them a guide appeared to me. It was a friend of mine from my freshman year of high school, a guy named Gabe Moses. It was only later on reflection that I realized it was a version of the archangel Gabriel ("Gabe" from the people of Moses...). He was there to guide me in what might be called "soul recovery" from those dark places in the underworld.

The same thing could be true if you happen to dream of a celebrity or other known person in the outer world but who you never met in real life. In these cases it can be helpful to think of who and what that person represents symbolically. This might help in interpreting the dream or making sense of the experience.

I've also had lots of dreams from various occult teachers or people whose works I've intensely studied. Just because you go to sleep for the night doesn't mean you stop learning. Sometimes it's just a trip to Night School.

Sometimes too, I've dreamed of my late mother. Early after her death in 2008 she visited me many times to comfort me, and in one case to show me what she was doing in her next phase of learning. Yet she has come back other times too, when I've needed advice or help. There is an intense quality to these dreams that might not be present in other dreams of the deceased, where I am processing memories or things from the day, etc. Knowing how to sense what part of the astral plane you are accessing in a dream can thus give you a better handle on knowing who is who in your dreams.

Your mileage may vary of course, but these things have helped me.

Sweet dreams and I hope you are getting enough zzzs.

Justin Patrick Moore

replies.title: Re: Dreams
replies.content:
Charles Godfrey Leland wrote about this at some length in his Gypsy Sorcery and Fortune-Telling (1891). His view was that he had "an other mind, or a second Me in my brain" [p. 14], which dreamt his dreams when he slept. "This Dream Artist is, to judge by his works, a very different kind of person from Me. We are not sympathetic. ..... Our characters are radically different. Why has he a mind so utterly unlike mine? His tastes, his thoughts, dispositions, and petty peculiarities are all unlike mine. If we belonged to the same club, I should never talk with him." [p. 165] "Within our self, there dwells a more mysterious Me, in some respects a more gifted Self. ..... [It] knows things hidden from Me, and can do what I cannot. ..... [It] actually often acts against ME--it plays fast and loose with me--it tries to deceive me." [p. 163]

There is a lot more about this "second me" in his chapters XI and XII, and it is clear that Leland supposes people generally have such a "second me."

In some of his later works, Leland expands this insight into multiple "other selves" in (most?) people, including at least one self of the opposite sex [see his The Alternate Sex (1904)]. And some of these other selves can even do magic ...

I think Leland was essentially right about all this. In most people, of course, it seems that these alternate selves work together fairly harmoniously so as to get the body they inhabit through life safely. In rare cases, however, they do not cooperate well, and in extreme cases the body they inhabit exhibits a disorder that used to be called Multiple Personality Disorder (now Dissociative Identy Disorder).

But I quickly figured out what was happening when I decided to yell at them, and quacked instead.) In both cases the events of the dreams were confirmed when I woke.

replies.title: Re: Dreams
replies.content:
There are several ideas about where dreams come from. Here are a few in no particular order.

1. Random discharge of nervous system energy. Sorts out unprocessed thoughts, along with random bursts of noise. Memories could be activated and will be woven into the random scenario.

2. Meaningful subconscious processing of personal experiences, perhaps linking with archetypes. Memories would fit into a very synchronistic story rich in insight.

3. Messaging from our own higher self or another beneficial spiritual forces. Memories will be activated if relevant to the meaningful scenario. If message is beyond conscious understanding, emotional theme might be all we retain.

Maybe your "layers of waking up" is type 2 or 3?

4. Intrusion by a disruptive trickster or intentionally malicious entity. Not intended for our own good. Confusion and manipulation may be part of the attack.

5. Random encounters in the astral realm, as an astral pinball bouncing around. Could be with good, bad, or neutral forces. Memories may be randomly activated. With no focused direction of astral path, scenario will be random and we paste on whatever labels seem to help it make sense. Bleed through for some other version of life could be present.

Maybe your "relabeled my life and loved ones" is type 4 or 5?

comments.title: Spirits
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

The last few years have led me to try and regain my sense of enchantment from my youth, as I remember being very favorable towards magic in my youth. I have a couple of questions for you since you are so knowledgeable on the subject, I apologize in advance if they are stupid or naive.

1) I have always had an affinity with animals, with wild animals showing me less fear than I would expect, and people-friendly animals like pets always wanting attention from me to the point of not leaving me alone if I visit someone with pets. I have also noticed that there are people with the opposite state, where even the friendliest animals on the planet want nothing to do with them no matter how nice they are being toward that animal. Is there a magical explanation for how animals views individual people?

2) I have struggled with alcoholism my entire adult life and until recently have just accepted the addictive chemical explanation, but in reading about the way spirits can influence or behaviors, I have started to wonder if there isn't some piece to it from that. It certainly feels like I am being controlled by a compulsion I don't understand, and all the worst parts of me are being brought to the surface when I fall off the wagon. Is there any truth to the idea that those of us who struggle with addiction are somehow influenced with malign spirits? And if there is, do you know of any practices that could help prevent this outside influence? It's hard to articulate to anyone who doesn't suffer from addiction, but it truly feels like I've been taken over by Mr. Hyde when it gets going.

Thank you for all the teaching you do.

Sub

replies.title: Re: Spirits
replies.content:
1) Animals are more perceptive than we are, since they don't have chattering minds getting in the way. They sense the emotional tone of a person very readily, where too many humans let themselves be fooled.

2) That's a complex issue. On the one hand, alcohol makes it easier to perceive the lower reaches of the astral plane; that's why it's used in religious rituals such as the Mass, in small, carefully controlled doses, but it's also one of the things that causes drunkenness to become so destructive, because the lower astral has a lot of crap in it and that can affect you when you're intoxicated. On the other, in vino veritas -- if you're bottling up negative feelings and reactions inside yourself, alcohol will let them out. Daily journaling to release those feelings, and potentially the Order of Spiritual Alchemy exercises (available for free download here), can help you release your own stock of unresolved crap.

replies.title: Re: Spirits
replies.content: 2) I don't know if it's "True," that malign spirits are involved in addiction; and, from personal experience I believe it can be a helpful lens through which to view things. When I tried believing that my cravings were an external force, I could respond to them rather than feeling compelled by them. Similar to how you can have a conversation with yourself in journaling, I eventually got to the point where I could have the physical craving for alcohol, think the thought of when/where/how I would acquire some, and then stop and talk to that urge (even seeing it behind me or in the corner of my vision.) It was incredibly powerful and allowed me to implement many other strategies to manage things, including doing the Order of Spiritual Alchemy work.

comments.title: Question on Sphere of Protection
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I'm a long-time practitioner of the LBRP currently in the process of "converting" to the SoP. I have been following the 8-phase program that you provide here at Ecosophia. At this point, I have reached phase 5, incorporating the calling of Earth in my practice.

I have a question for you: though I am careful to do the practice "by the book", i.e. exactly as you describe it, over the weeks certain impressions/sensations have started to come up all by themselves.

Thus, upon invoking each of the elements, the symbol of the element enlarges to become some kind of bay window allowing me to watch, feel, hear (etc.) the scenery of the quarter. Breathing in, I see/feel a stream of the element (wind, heat, water, ...) coming out of the scenery towards me, surrounding me; in mind's eye, I visualize my aura then changing to the color of the element.

When I banish, I see the imbalances of the nature of the element as some kind of "dust" of the color of the element, only darkened. As I breathe out, that dark dust leaves my aura and goes through the bay window into the scenery to be dissolved in the element; I then see the elemental color already present in my aura glowing and shining brighter than before the departure of the dust.

This twofold process happens "in the good days", i.e. when I really am into the practice. But it came about spontaneously. However, you have warned on many occasions against tampering with the practices, as it is a sign that our subconscious is trying to alter the practices in order to make them ineffective, i.e. really to avoid being affected, changed by them!

My question, therefore, is really the following: inasmuch as the impressions/sensations above are not a part of the "official script" of the SoP, is it simply me subconsciously adding my two cents to the practice to avoid doing it exactly as I am told? Or can they be considered an acceptable development I can allow to unfold?

Thank you for your answer!

replies.title: Re: Question on Sphere of Protection
replies.content: Let them unfold. The SoP is more flexible than many other rituals; I don't recommend changing anything for the first year or so, but if things emerge in addition to the things covered in the instruction, keep note of them but let them happen.

replies.title: Re: Question on Sphere of Protection
replies.content: If I could piggyback on this a bit, I have been doing the LBRP/MPR daily for about 6 years now. I've given the SOP some consideration (I'm doing the FHR work and have an eye on the adjacent material that exclusively uses the SOP). I was wondering if switching would negatively affect the work I've done with my energy body to date, or what other types of changes I could expect if I decided to switch?

replies.title: Re: Question on Sphere of Protection
replies.content: People who come to the SoP after years of practice of the LBRP/MP usually start out feeling disappointed, as though the SoP wasn't doing anything at all. It's a gentler and subtler ritual; I've come to prefer it, but it's not for everyone. My experience is that in general, the benefits you got from working with the LBRP/MP won't go away, but it'll take you a while to get used to the very different SoP effects. Have you done divination about the matter?

comments.title: Magic and the Long Descent
comments.content:
Greetings, and many condolences.

Although I understand that you and some others fare better with distraction and work while grieving, I am nevertheless in awe that you continue to host and post to the same extent at this time, and so soon.

I have been thinking about this problem or question for a few weeks, and your post of the cover of The Long Descent seems to make this the perfect occasion to ask.

Economic and environmental decline makes a lot of things more difficult, and can mean that getting results from workings is harder than it was. Some authors in alternative spirituality would say this is just a personal mindset issue that requires positive thinking, but I am sure you see this as a real external change in the world too, and one that you probably foresaw many years ago, that tightening material and economic conditions would reduce what was easily possible. Is there anything we can do in adjusting practices to help?

In my country the housing rental market is very difficult. I need to move because my current place is not very good for my health needs, and I would be able to do more in a number of ways which would benefit my health, income and outgoings. A few years ago, any working to help with moving home and finding a good place, would have been just like an extra good luck charm, an icing on the cake. But in my country now, if you are not a couple with two full time earners or two good private pensions, you have a low chance of getting the sort of home I mean, and you may have to attend literally dozens of viewings at short notice (which get in the way of work and can wear out someone whose health is not good). Five or ten years ago I could walk into these places and get the first one I applied for. The compromise I am making for the moment is staying where I am, rather than taking somewhere less suitable which would have some of the same problems or worse than what I am facing currently. A more suitable place to live would enable me to help myself in a lot more ways.

I feel more like a statistic subject to historical forces than an individual with agency now.

Maybe some of this is also magical doldrums, like another person mentioned earlier today, but to me it seems like something at least as much on this plane as others

replies.title: Re: Magic and the Long Descent
replies.content:
1) Since Sara's final illness began in October, I had the chance to do a lot of my grieving in advance, and I also spent much more time than I like sitting around and waiting for the inevitable. Getting back to work feels like getting out of a cave and into the sunlight.

2) This is indeed a real, external change in the world. The sort of teaching that insists you can have what you want if you just want it enough flourishes during prosperous times but tends to be discarded once hard times hit; here in the US, for example, the 1920s saw lots of people convince themselves that prosperity could be had by simply having the right attitude. Then the Great Depression happened, and they learned otherwise.

You can still work on getting a more suitable place, but it's going to take more patience and possibly some very robust magic. On the other hand, it may be that your country's in the same kind of speculative real estate bubble that mine's having right now, in which case things may right themselves spectacularly in a year or two...

replies.title: Re: Magic and the Long Descent
replies.content:

Where can I learn more about suitable forms of very robust magic? I have been practising the SOP and planetary charity for several years. I feel a bit worn out at the moment: I haven't had the sort of successes for a few months that usually feel like they feed back in to magic.

With the amount of serious illness that I am hearing about recently, often friends aging parents getting sicker earlier than they thought they would, and finding it difficult to access care to the standards they once expected, I often think back to your old posts about the fall of the Soviet Union and the increase in death rates and infrastructure decay then. It feels quite close, this isn't just on the news, and I think this contributes to my feeling worn out and there being fewer possibilities.

I also wonder if it is worth paying more attention to gods who were prominent during other times of decay like the post-Roman era? I think that makes a lot of sense for tough, robust people who could take to those conditions. I'm not sure about it for people like me who need a bit of civilization to get by, even if not as acutely as some.

replies.title: Re: Magic and the Long Descent
replies.content:
The most robust system of magic I've practiced is the classic Golden Dawn system. If you want to get into that, plan on 30 minutes to an hour a day of practice for the next ten to twenty years. You can do astonishing things with it, but only if you put in the time and hard work to get good at it.

As for gods, we don't yet know which ones will rise to prominence in the deindustrial era, so it's a crapshoot.

In any case, wishing everyone hear best of luck on your individual quests and paths.

Justin Patrick Moore

replies.title: Bells and sounds

I am very new to this site and your work, and I do not know how you will take such posts (just delete it if you find them obnoxious), but anybody who is interested in this topic (or the sacredness of sound in general) may enjoy the work of a Sufi named Hazrat Inayat Khan:

https://wahiduddin.net/mv2/II/II_0.htm

From another part of his work:

6. Bells and Gongs
The secret of the religious custom of having gongs and bells in temples and churches lies in the great science of the Hindus, which is Mantra Yoga. In the first place, this custom unites several religions, since bells are rung in Christian churches, in the temples of the Hindus, and in Buddhist pagodas. Many think that it is a call to prayer, but from a mystical point of view it is something more than that. The idea of the mystic is to make his heart capable of resonance, that every voice that rises on earth or descends from heaven may have its re-echo in his heart. The Sufi prepares himself by his exercises of zikr and fikr to make his heart capable of producing that resonance that may be caused on earth or descend from heaven. When the centers of the body and faculties of the mind are prepared to produce that resonance, then they respond to every sound, and every time the bell is rung it has its re-echo in the heart of the mystic, and every center of his being begins to think of God and to feel God. Vibration is a greater stimulant than wine. Wine gives intoxication to the brain, but vibration produces ecstasy in the heart. Therefore Sufis have called it wine.

The custom of having flowers in the house of prayer and the custom of burning incense in the place of worship also exists in almost all religions and has existed in almost all periods. Color and beauty also have a power, an influence upon the mind and body, and those who can enjoy the beauty of color and the delicacy of the flower receive help by the way of the eyes. Its effect opens the heart, which then responds to the blessing from above. This shows that the beauty of the earth can be best used to obtain the blessing from heaven. The perfume of flowers or incense has a deeper effect still, because color and beauty are only reflected upon the heart through the eyes, but perfume and incense, rising through the breath, touch the heart, making it capable of spiritual exaltation. But nothing has a greater influence on the human soul than sound. Therefore hymns are sung in all churches, prayers are offered, and chants are recited, all in order to wake the spirit within to life, which enables the soul to respond to the perfection of God.

replies.title: Re: Bells and sounds
replies.content:
Thank you for this. It gave me something to meditate over. I'll share my subsequent thoughts.

I learned recently about an obscure branch of science called Cymatics, which is the study of the effects of vibration - for example the patterns created in sand when placed on a loudspeaker playing a constant tone. Some sound frequencies make orderly patterns, some make geometric shapes (many look a lot like sacred geometry) and some are a disordered mess.

Also related to what you mention is "sympathetic resonance" - an effect where one vibrating string will make a nearby stationary one also start vibrating at the same frequency, but reduced amplitude. Some musical instruments such as the sitar and the hurdy-gurdy use the principle for extra 'drone' strings.

I guess my original question was why the sound of a bell should be more effective at purification than, say, scraping one piece of metal down another (and if thinking of that sound sends an uncomfortable shiver down your spine, it's probably a good indication it's not a purifying sound!), but I imagine it may be due to resonance - the ringing of a bell is a standing wave set up in the structure of the metal, so it's like the metal is 'singing' over and over again. It may be that the tones created by bells are just right to set up their surrounding area with a sympathetic resonance which re-aligns it with the natural harmonic frequencies of the world.

JMG has mentioned it on here before, but some occult traditions state that demons are left-over entities from a previous universe, who became trapped due to their imbalances. Maybe I've read too much sci-fi, but this sounds a lot like prideful species in some sci-fi books I've read who try to escape the heat death and 'big crunch' of their universe by retreating into a timeless pocket dimension to emerge into the new universe. But the physical laws of a new universe won't be the same as the old one, so the 'old' maths and logic will no longer apply (what JMG calls "2+2 = petunia" logic - ie. demon logic makes no sense to natives of this universe and vice versa). So the 'pure' tones of a bell, resonating in line with the mathematics of this universe, would sound just as awful as scraping metal to a demon from a previous universe. Kind of makes you think, when you hear that places like churches and Shinto shrines have been asked to stop ringing their bells by their neighbours, citing "noise pollution"...

Mr. Crow

replies.title: Crowley and Bucky
replies.content:
An overview of Crowley's life and magickal thinking:
https://hermetic.com/eidolons/a-guide-to-the-study-of-thelema

An overview of Fuller's life and ideas (he didn't offer a system of magick):
https://exhibits.stanford.edu/bucky/feature/who-was-bucky-fuller

Crowley wanted to lead people to a spiritual experience that would connect with their inner self and maximize their free will, regardless of society's rules. He always said much of his material was channeled. He combined ideas from many sources into his own work. He tried hard to build a personal mystique around his work. He was deliberately esoteric and obscure and shocking.

Fuller anted to lead people to a thought process that would guide designs which shape things, like buildings and cars, to enhance human life. He tried to find scientific principles that would always apply. He tried hard to explain his unique concepts and thought process. He was unintentionally esoteric and obscure. He thought when people realized how reasonable his message was they would abandon lesser systems in society and life.

He only told of a spiritual experience much later in his life. He had earlier described how he was at a lake, going to kill himself as useless so his wife and young daughter could get the insurance money. He realized his life could be of benefit to others if he kept on living. Later in his life he said there was an angelic visitation or visionary experience that brought this message to him.

comments.title: Q
comments.content:
So I've been going through the kundalini rising event for a year now, and when I pray to st Michael, I get a tingling/heat sensation in my hands (which are energy sensitive anyway since I got initiated into reiki).

But recently I was reading about St Michael, and it seems that he prefigures christianity, and there are speculations that he was a Canaanite storm god repurposed into a much brighter figure.
Link: https://michaelruark.blog/2019/02/11/archangel-michael-the-wrathful-aspect-of-baal/

It made me wonder how the same spirit could be aggressive, violent or a saintly presence based on the projections of the witness and the society they dwell in. Gods and their natures and powers seem to correlate with the nature of the culture and the people, and that does make me wonder if these symbols are unconscious projections of internal impulses of the mind, rather than actual external divine spirits.

Every culture/religion creates its metaphysics to organise its libidinal energies, and so the symbols end up with power that influences the people right back, but are there actual external conscious beings that take up the archetypical symbol, or are we merely just talking to sub-agents of our own individual and collective minds.

Part of this is visible in the way that in cultures/societies which don't conceive of the importance of personal gods (early taoism, early buddhism, some versions of hinduism/vedanta), get by just fine and don't seem to have any interaction with forces they don't conceive in their metaphysics. Gods don't make themselves known so to speak.

Similarly, with reincarnation/rebirth, even though I've had a past life experience, I am not sure what it exactly means. Growing up hindu, it's hard to say if the archetypical imprinting of these notions are what are causing these memories (as a type of imagination, or tapping into some collective mind property of the world), or if they are a genuinely ontological property of the universe.

I know you've thought about these topics for decades besides reading plenty of Jung, Schopenhauer etc so I'm curious what you think and how you came to be sure there are more individuated centres of consciousness than just the ones we see with our main senses.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content: The human mind is unable to prove truths about the universe. That's the rock on which every system of rationalism breaks, and the current version is no more exempt from that fate than any other. It is therefore impossible to prove whether or not the universe includes individuated centers of consciousness that cannot be perceived with the material senses. Yet the universe appears to function as though such centers exist, and many people have had what appear to be encounters with disembodied beings of various kinds; I've had such experiences, and so have many people who didn't believe in such things before they had such an encounter for themselves. (My father, who is about as psychic as a brick, had such an experience -- I spent a long phone call calming him down!) That being the case, since it's impossible to prove things one way or another, it seems most sensible to me to accept the hypothesis that gods, spirits, and the like exist, and to act accordingly.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content: What is your view on why Jung considered these mental phenomena deep in the unconscious as symbolic structures and didnt go all the way to consider them actual gods / spirits?

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content: That's a common misunderstanding of Jung. If you read his writings you'll find that what he said was that since he was writing as a psychologist, he was only going to discuss the archetypes as psychological phenomena -- he never denied that they could also have an objective, metaphysical reality.

comments.title: Three Questions
comments.content:
Once again Mr. Greer, my deepest condolences. And it is good to see you back.

1. Do you think there is an occult/demonic dimension to the fixation on Russia on the part of Faustian elites? I think it might have been you who termed the phenomenon "Suicide By Russia", but it seems to be such a consistent pattern that I wonder if there be some Occult dimension to this? I'm thinking about how the "Wotan" archetype essentially puppeted Hitler into making a beeline for his own Gotterdammerung, and I'm also reminded of how Dion Fortune's "Tracks in Space" can lay down astral patterns that are repeated- and this might go back much further, all the way to the Tollense Valley Battlefield. It's just striking to me, since by all rights anyone with any common sense should be able to see that this is insanity. Why keep doing it? Over hundreds of years?


2. Do you think there is any occult dimension to the Trans-identity-activism? Because to me that seems to have seized the steering wheel of the LGBT community and aimed at the nearest cliff- and I have many friends in that community that I fear for, should the backlash be harsh enough. But they seem to A) be acting exactly as their enemies paint them(right down to increasingly disturbing reports of pedophilia), and B) acting as if they are literally demonic.

I mean this in the terms that people who suddenly seem to, out of nowhere, identify as trans, seem to be taken over by an entirely different personality. Add to the confusion that there seems to be powerful archetypes driving this, especially in social media, and the fact that it seems to be much more pronounced in transwomen rather than trans men, and I really do find myself confused as to what's going on.

One thought occurs in that "What you contemplate, you imitate", and many isolated young men fine themselves addicted to(and therefore contemplating) porn, therefore contemplating a hypersexually distorted archetype that bears little resemblance to actual women, which would be a crack in the psychic defenses that a demon could enter. Or be invited into. Thoughts?

3. What are the differences, and different purposes of the Greater Hexagram rituals, and the Supreme Pentagram? I have heard that the Hexagram is better for invoking planetary energies, but that's about it, and have been finding it difficult to obtain further information.

As always, many thanks.

Matt

replies.title: Re: Three Questions
replies.content:
1) Ha! No, I didn't invent "suicide by Russia," and I don't think I'd heard it before; I laughed very hard when I read that, because it's both hilarious and accurate. I hope some of my Russian readers pick it up and pass it around in their homeland, because I think it would suit the Russian sense of humor.

To my mind Oswald Spengler explains suicide by Russia better than any occult philosopher. The entire Faustian consciousness is obsessed with the idea of expansion into infinite distance; one of the forms that obsession has taken has long been the idea of European military expansion into the vast distances of Russia. The difficulties are twofold. First, Europe is a cramped little subcontinent full of little nations with overinflated ideas of their importance, while Russia is as close to limitless space as you can get on this planet; thus European invasions always fail because the Europeans simply can't grasp the distances involved. Second, of course, Russia is tolerably well stocked with Russians, who object to being expanded over, and tend to express that objection very forcefully indeed. (Cue the cannon fire in Tchaikovsky's 1812 Overture.) So time after time, Faustian Europe goes to war against infinity and gets handed its rump on a platter. The consequences have gotten worse and worse for Europe each time it's tried; one of these days the end result will involve Russian troops in Lisbon.

2) Yes, there are serious occult issues here. First of all, since there are so many human bodies these days, very few souls have enough time between bodies to process their experiences, and so people are getting born with dim memories of their previous bodies. Second, the popularity of "futanari" and "shemale" pornography -- basically, women with penises -- has generated a potent image on the lower astral into which the weak-minded can be drawn. Third, yes, masturbation to pornography by young men who have no spiritual lives and no protections against demonic activity can open them to obsession by noxious spirits. Those three factors and others have created a real mess, and yes, the blowback is likely to be pretty ghastly.

3) The hexagram ritual invokes planetary energies, the pentagram ritual invokes elemental energies. That's the difference between them; if you want to invoke (say) Venus you do the one, if you want to invoke (say) Air you do the other. My book Circles of Power covers all this in detail.

comments.title: Note on Your Predictions for a Future American Empire
comments.content:
Two things that will be of interest to you, from a mytho-historical perspective and ideas of cyclical time:

1. The recent conflict in Ukraine has revealed that the American military is second-rate (and declining) when it comes to the ability to logistically supply and manage large-scale warfare between massed armies. Due to low recruitment totals, a greater reliance on poorer quality people, and poor training, the mass of the American military is becoming outright incompetent. The last sections of the military that still fight well are the hyper-elite special forces, which are not designed for mass engagements.

2. The American navy being beaten now at sea, and strapped for cash, it's not hard to imagine a turning away from the sea. This has happened before, in fact, when often the United States government ignored blue water navigation and infrastructure to focus on inland navigation. Isolationism, which would almost certainly entail a reduction in sea power, is also a popular sentiment.

Most people know that the Native Tribes of North America were excellent, lifelong warfighters who fought asymmetrically. What most are only dimly aware of is that in massed battles, those advantages went into total reverse and the Natives suffered disproportionally high losses and were slaughtered. They did not know how to fight large systemic warfare and engagements. The Native tribes had great /warriors/ and terrible /soldiers/. The American military and general populace is looking to imitate that pattern.

Also, for reasons that have never been explained and, in fact, are quite baffling, even when (in the case of the North American tribes), the natives of the Americas were great inland navigators and boat builders, or lived close to the sea, they were serenely uninterested in saltwater exploration. Mainlanders did not even take an interest in the accessible Caribbean. Will future Americans also be stubborn land-lubbers? Seems possible.

Funny how the land itself, across deep time, seems to be bending people to its own design.

comments.title: Delayed Manifestation
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Here is my question, if you don't mind answering:

For some reason, I have throughout my life always been significantly delayed in achieving/attaining anything that I really wanted, whether that was getting my driver's license or driving my first car, moving out of my house, first girlfriend, first job, or anything else (the latter items, for instance, took 1-3 decades longer than everybody else I knew). My first question is whether you can give a generic explanation as to why this is the case --it *definitely* was not due to a lack of desire or motivation or work ethic on my part.

As for the more important question, I am now nearing 50 and really only have two personal goals in my life--one professional and one spiritual. Do you have any practical advice as to how I can speed up attaining those goals or how I can make sure I actually achieve them and, if I achieve them, how to surpass them?

Also, on a completely unrelated topic, have you ever read Carlos Castaneda's books? While the consensus is that the events within it are pure fiction, I am curious if the various things he discusses have any semblance to reality or if they are complete BS.

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Delayed Manifestation
replies.content:
1) It could be any number of things, some of which you can influence, some of which you can't. If you happen to know a good astrologer, they might be able to help you by studying your natal chart.

2) Since I know essentially nothing about you, your situation, or your goals, no, there's no advice I can offer. In this business, one size definitely does not fit all.

3) Castaneda was an interesting cat. The events in his books are pure fiction but the philosophy is not -- he knew his range around quite a lot of esoteric traditions, and put a great deal of wisdom in don Juan's mouth.
